chunk_summary: Management of extensive skin infections in oedematous SAM includes systemic antibiotics, anti-staphylococcal coverage, wound care, and monitoring for complications like dehydration and hypothermia.
learning_objectives: Understand the risks of extensive skin infections in oedematous SAM and their systemic implications.
Learn appropriate antibiotic regimens, including anti-staphylococcal coverage, for complicated SAM cases.
Master wound care principles to prevent further skin damage and infection.
Recognize and manage associated complications such as dehydration and hypothermia.
key_facts: Extensive skin breakdown in oedematous SAM can seed bacteremia even in afebrile children.
Standard parenteral antibiotics include ampicillin plus gentamicin; add cloxacillin for staphylococcal coverage in purulent or spreading lesions.
Escalate antibiotic therapy at 48–72 hours if no improvement, guided by local antibiogram and culture results.
Wound care includes gentle cleaning, barrier dressings (e.g., petrolatum or zinc-based), and avoiding caustic antiseptics.
Monitor for dehydration and hypothermia due to increased insensible fluid losses from open lesions.
reference_quote: Treat weeping kwashiorkor dermatosis, ulceration, spreading cellulitis, or abscess as systemic infection risk and reassess antibiotic coverage early. (National Health Mission, Participant manual for facility-based care of severe acute malnutrition)
document_key_topics: Extensive skin infection management in SAM
Wound care principles for fragile skin
Monitoring and managing complications in SAM
